data.title: Quant snapshot on cannabis names as Trump plans reclassification
data.description:
Cannabis stocks rallied sharply on Wednesday after reports that the Trump administration is preparing to formally reclassify marijuana, potentially as soon as this week. The change would place cannabis alongside less-restricted substances like ketamine or certain codeine products.

The rally was led by Tilray Brands (TLRY), which surged about 14%, while Canopy Growth (CGC) jumped roughly 21% and Curaleaf (CURLF) climbed around 26%, marking some of the strongest gains in the sector. Other cannabis names also advanced, with Aurora Cannabis (ACB) and Cronos (CRON) trading higher alongside U.S. multi-state operators and cannabis ETFs.

Cannabis-related ETFs: AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF (MSOS), AdvisorShares MSOS 2x Daily ETF (MSOX), Amplify Alternative Harvest ETF (MJ), Roundhill Cannabis ETF (WEED), Cambria Cannabis ETF (TOKE), Amplify Seymour Cannabis ETF (CNBS), AdvisorShares Pure Cannabis ETF (YOLO).

Let us do a quick Seeking Alpha check on the quant ratings of these cannabis names.

  • MariMed (MRMD), Quant rating: Hold 3.21.
  • Cronos Group (CRON), Quant rating: Hold 3.13.
  • Vext Science (VEXTF), Quant rating: Hold 3.10.
  • TerrAscend (TSNDF), Quant rating: Hold 3.01.
  • Trulieve Cannabis (TCNNF), Quant rating: Hold 2.90.
  • Curaleaf Holdings (CURLF), Quant rating: Hold 2.89.
  • Green Thumb Industries (GTBIF), Quant rating: Hold 2.74.
  • Cresco Labs (CRLBF), Quant rating: Hold 2.64.
  • Aurora Cannabis (ACB), Quant rating: Hold 2.62.
  • Jushi Holdings (JUSHF), Quant rating: Hold 2.59.
  • Canopy Growth Corporation (CGC), Quant rating: Hold 2.58.
  • SNDL Inc. (SNDL), Quant rating: Hold 2.57.
  • Glass House Brands (GLASF), Quant rating: Sell 1.81.
  • Tilray Brands (TLRY), Quant rating: Strong Sell 1.36.

Euro Area composite PMI falls to 48.6 as services slip, manufacturing edges higher in April
data.description:
• Composite PMI in the Euro Area decreased to 48.60 points in April from 50.70 points in March of 2026.
  • Manufacturing PMI in the Euro Area increased to 52.20 points in April from 51.60 points in March of 2026. 
  • Services PMI in the Euro Area decreased to 47.40 points in April from 50.20 points in March of 2026. 

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (DAX)

Currency: (EUR:USD)

data.title: American Airlines and Alaska Air explore partnerships options - report
data.description:
American Airlines (AAL) and Alaska Air Group (ALK) are exploring revenue-sharing agreements and other strategic partnerships as carriers seek to scale amid rising costs and competition, according to a media report. 

The talks include adding Alaska to American’s existing joint business arrangements, including its transatlantic partnership with IAG SA unit British Airways and its Pacific venture with Japan Airlines, Bloomberg News reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

Such arrangements, subject to U.S. Department of Transportation approval, allow airlines to coordinate schedules and pricing on certain routes and share revenue.

A merger was also discussed as part of negotiations over a stronger partnership but did not advance, according to the report.

Broader arrangements could give American greater reach on the US West Coast and strengthen connectivity through Alaska’s Seattle hub, while providing the smaller carrier with deeper access to lucrative long-haul markets as it pushes global growth

American Airlines and Alaska Air did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

U.S. airlines are navigating  higher fuel costs linked to conflict in the Middle East. 

The report says the partnership would enable American, which is trying to boost sales and pay down debt, to better defend itself against more profitable rivals United Airlines (UAL) and Delta Air Lines (DAL) in key markets. 

Alaska Air also owns Hawaiian Airlines, acquired in 2024, and operates a predominantly Boeing (BA) fleet.

data.title: Lufthansa cuts 20,000 'unprofitable' flights to save on jet fuel costs
data.description:
Lufthansa (DLAKF) said it will remove 20,000 short-haul flights through October as a result of jet fuel prices doubling since the outbreak of the Iran war.

This will reduce the number of unprofitable short-haul flights across the German airline's network, cutting its summer capacity by 1% of available seat kilometers (a measure of passenger carrying capacity).

Lufthansa (DLAKF) will save about 40,000 metric tons of jet fuel with this move and its supply is secured for the coming weeks. 

It is also accelerating the consolidation of European networks within its hub airlines — Lufthansa Airlines, SWISS, Austrian Airlines, Brussels Airlines, and ITA Airways. It canceled routes from Frankfurt to Bydgoszcz and Rzeszów (Poland) as well as Stavanger (Norway).

In addition, Lufthansa (DLAKF) is implementing measures to ensure stable fuel supply, including the physical procurement of jet fuel and price hedging.

Jet fuel prices surged from ~$99 per barrel at February-end to ~$200/bbl at the start of April, easing to a little over $180/bbl currently.

The International Energy Agency last week warned that Europe could run out of jet fuel in about six weeks and airlines would start reducing flights.

"We also estimated that by the end of May we could start to see some cancellations in Europe for lack of jet fuel," Willie Walsh, director general of the International Air Transport Association, said. 

All but one of the world's 20 largest airlines canceled flights scheduled for May, according to aviation analytics firm Cirium. These include Delta (DAL), United (UAL), American Airlines (AAL), Air Canada (ACDVF), Emirates, Qatar Airways, Air China (AIRYY), British Airways (ICAGY) and Air France-KLM (AFLYY).

Some airlines are also slowing their plans to add more seats and routes to help rein in costs.

-- The Associated Press contributed to this report.

data.title: Sanofi tops Q1 estimates on Dupixent strength, backs full-year outlook
data.description:
Sanofi (SNY) reported better-than-expected first-quarter results, recording double-digit sales and business EPS growth.

Earnings per share, excluding some items, were €1.88 ($2.20), up by 14.0% at constant exchange rates and 5.0% at actual exchange rates, and higher than the consensus forecast of €1.8. Sales increased by 13.6% at CER, +6.2% at actual rates, to €10.51B.

Dupixent, the firm’s top-selling skin and asthma drug, had a strong start to the year, with sales rising 30.8% to €4.2B. Vaccines sales rose by 2.1% to €1.3B, benefiting from Heplisav-B.

Sanofi (SNY) affirmed its guidance for the year, with sales projected to grow by a high single-digit percentage and business EPS to grow slightly faster than sales, at constant exchange rates.

According to Bloomberg Intelligence senior industry analyst John Murphy, the company appears on track to exceed that outlook. “Yet the focus is likely to center more on potential future strategic changes once new CEO Belén Garijo assumes the role on April 29,” Murphy added.

The results provide a lift for incoming chief executive Belén Garijo. In February, Sanofi’s board decided not to renew the director mandate of former CEO Paul Hudson.

Garijo, a former senior executive at the company, is expected to revitalize the drugmaker’s pipeline of new medicines. Sanofi is facing the upcoming patent expiry of Dupixent early next decade and has yet to outline a convincing strategy to replace revenue from the blockbuster treatment.

data.title: CVB Financial gives mixed Q1 numbers
data.description:
• CVB Financial press release (CVBF): Q1 GAAP EPS of $0.38 beats by $0.01.
  • Revenue of $132.12M (+4.3% Y/Y) misses by $0.94M.
  • Net Earnings of $51.0 million, or $0.38 per share
  • Return on Average Assets of 1.33%
  • Net Interest Margin of 3.44%
  • Pretax pre-provision income[1] grew by $4.0 million or 6% from Q1 of 2025
  • Net interest income grew by $7.4 million, or 6.7% from Q1 of 2025
  • Net interest margin of 3.44% increased by 13 basis points from Q1 of 2025
  • Cost of funds decreased to 0.97% from 1.04 % in Q1 of 2025

data.title: Germany's manufacturing and service sectors deteriorate in April
data.description:
• Manufacturing PMI in Germany decreased to 51.20 points in April from 52.20 points last month, lower than estimates of 51.30.
  • Composite PMI in Germany decreased to 48.30 points in April from 51.90 points, below the consensus of 51.1
  • Services PMI in Germany decreased to 46.90 points in April from 50.90 points, below estimates of 50.30.

data.title: France's manufacturing sector improves in April
Servicenow raises 2026 subscription revenue outlook to $15.735B-$15.775B while integrating Armis
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ServiceNow (NOW) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "There's a lot of noise out there, so let's get straight to the point." (Chairman & CEO William McDermott) Management positioned ServiceNow as "the AI control tower for business reinvention" and cited "a $28 billion RPO business that's growing at 23.5% year-over-year," alongside "16 deals greater than $5 million in NNACV and 5 deals greater than $10 million in NNACV." (Chairman & CEO McDermott)

  • McDermott said the Armis acquisition "closed earlier than expected," and described a combined security stack: "Armis asset visibility plus Veza's identity governance plus ServiceNow's business context, CMDB equals a unified end-to-end security stack." (Chairman & CEO McDermott)

  • "Q1 subscription revenues were $3.671 billion, growing 19% year-over-year in constant currency." (President & CFO Gina Mastantuono) She added that results included "about a 75 basis point headwind from delayed closings of several large on-premise deals in the Middle East due to the ongoing conflict in the region." (President & CFO Mastantuono)

  • "AI Control Tower also continues to build momentum with average deal sizes more than doubling quarter-over-quarter in Q1." (President & CFO Mastantuono) She also highlighted capital return: "In Q1, we executed a $2 billion accelerated share repurchase and bought back approximately 20.2 million shares." (President & CFO Mastantuono)

Outlook

  • Management raised full-year subscription revenue guidance: "for 2026, we are raising our subscription revenues by $205 million at the midpoint to $15.735 billion to $15.775 billion." (President & CFO Mastantuono)

  • Profitability and cash flow guidance incorporated Armis integration: "We now expect subscription gross margin of 81.5% and operating margin of 31.5%" and "We expect free cash flow margin of 35%." (President & CFO Mastantuono)

  • Q2 guidance: "subscription revenues between $3.815 billion and $3.820 billion" and "cRPO growth of 19.5% on a constant currency basis." (President & CFO Mastantuono)

  • Versus last quarter’s call, management shifted from the prior full-year subscription revenue guide of "$15.53 billion to $15.57 billion" to the higher range above, and updated Armis’ margin impact language to near-term integration headwinds with an expectation that AI efficiencies and leverage "will normalize our operating and free cash flow margin expansion trajectory in 2027 and beyond." (President & CFO Mastantuono)

Financial results

  • "RPO ended the quarter at approximately $27.7 billion" and "Current RPO was $12.64 billion." (President & CFO Mastantuono) She also reported "Our renewal rate, inclusive of Moveworks was a strong 97%" and "we had approximately $4.2 billion of authorization remaining" under repurchases. (President & CFO Mastantuono)

  • Deal and customer metrics included "630 customers generating over $5 million in ACV" and "our largest net new logo deal ever at over $15 million." (President & CFO Mastantuono)

  • Product monetization commentary centered on Now Assist: "Now Assist continues to outperform expectations, putting it on a trajectory to exceed our $1 billion target for 2026." (President & CFO Mastantuono)

Q&A

  • Mark Murphy, JPMorgan: asked about Middle East deferrals and on-prem exposure; Chairman & CEO McDermott said, "we just beat and raised" and framed the impact as "a statement of fact that impacts slightly the guide," adding, "we don't have any long-term matter for those shareholders to be concerned with." President & CFO Mastantuono added, "we kept the full year guide."

  • Mark Murphy, JPMorgan: asked how AI revenue will be tracked under AI-native packaging; President, CPO & COO Amit Zavery said, "the incremental Assist part of it is what's going to be counted as our AI revenue." President & CFO Mastantuono said, "we have the exact same methodology."

  • Brad Zelnick, Deutsche Bank: pressed on differentiation amid orchestration competition; Chairman & CEO McDermott emphasized "context" and "95 billion workflows and more than 7 trillion transactions." President Zavery said, "we're going beyond just orchestration" and cited "Context Engine" and "autonomous workforce."

  • Gabriela Borges, Goldman Sachs: asked whether AI value leads customers to negotiate down the core; Chairman & CEO McDermott responded, "no, we're not getting negotiated down on the core" and tied core strength to AI, data fabric, and security expansion.

  • Peter Weed, Bernstein: asked how Autonomous Workforce complements Now Assist; President Zavery said a Level 1 AI specialist can reduce resolution time from "around 2 days" to "less than 20 minutes," and added, "we have around 20 different roles like this being delivered by May."

  • Keith Weiss, Morgan Stanley: questioned organic vs. inorganic contribution and acceleration, noting "The stock is down 12% after hours"; President & CFO Mastantuono said Veza and Pyramid were "very, very, very tiny" in Q1, and pointed to Now Assist expectations: "we're already talking about $1.5 billion now." Chairman & CEO McDermott said acquisitions were "not...intended to be a plug for a revenue gap."

  • Aleksandr Zukin, Wolfe Research: asked about global spending and AI anxiety; Chairman & CEO McDermott said customers are "very excited about AI" but "they don't exactly know what to do." President & CFO Mastantuono cited large-deal activity as proof points.

  • Samad Samana, Jefferies: asked about a new Chief Strategy Officer and priorities; President & CFO Mastantuono said, "Natural attrition," adding, "Krishna Gidwani is fantastic" and that integration is the top M&A priority.

  • Michael Turrin, Wells Fargo: asked whether M&A is offense/defense and how guidance treats new assets; Chairman & CEO McDermott said, "The company and the customer loves the acquisitions that we did" and called the control-tower vision "the biggest thing that I've seen the customer react to since I've been here since 2019." President & CFO Mastantuono said she is "being prudent with brand-new acquisitions" and sees "significant upside" as ramp progresses.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to skeptical on visibility and revisions, with direct challenge on market reaction: "The stock is down 12% after hours." (Keith Weiss, Morgan Stanley)

  • Management’s tone was confident and occasionally defensive on optics, using clarifiers such as "I want to be clear" and "we just beat and raised." (Chairman & CEO McDermott)

  • Versus the prior quarter, management maintained high confidence in AI monetization, but this call included more explicit Q&A pressure on organic/inorganic contribution and investor understanding.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 emphasized earlier-than-expected Armis close and formal guidance lift, while Q4 framed Armis and Veza as pending and stated, "we do not have a large-scale M&A on the road map." (Chairman & CEO McDermott, Q4)

  • Now Assist expectations escalated from Q4’s "tracking well towards our $1 billion-plus target for 2026" (President & CFO Mastantuono, Q4) to Q1’s discussion of "$1.5 billion" measured "the exact same way." (Chairman & CEO McDermott; President & CFO Mastantuono)

  • Analyst focus shifted from gross margin drivers and federal pipeline in Q4 to Q1 questions on Middle East timing, AI-native packaging measurement, and whether Q1 outperformance was organic.

Risks and concerns

  • Management flagged geopolitical risk and timing volatility: "delayed closings" in the Middle East created a Q1 headwind and influenced Q2 framing, particularly because sovereign cloud revenue is recognized as on-prem and "it's not linear or ratable." (President & CFO Mastantuono; Chairman & CEO McDermott)

  • Integration trade-offs were explicitly acknowledged: "we expect some near-term headwinds to margins as we integrate the business this year." (President & CFO Mastantuono)

  • Armis contract structure may affect cRPO mechanics: "They have a lot of T for C in their contracts, or termination for convenience. So not all of it can go into cRPO." (President & CFO Mastantuono)

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as "another quarter of outstanding execution" (President & CFO Mastantuono), paired with a raised 2026 subscription revenue outlook and expanded AI and security positioning following the earlier-than-expected Armis close. In Q&A, executives repeatedly emphasized that AI is embedded across SKUs, that Now Assist is measured as incremental AI contribution under the same methodology, and that near-term integration and geopolitical deal-timing headwinds were incorporated into guidance without changing management’s stated confidence in longer-term revenue acceleration and margin trajectory beyond 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
